title: From Control To Surrender
study: A Study In The Life Of King David
scriptureForWeek: 2 Samuel 11 and 12 cover the events of David’s sin with Bathsheba, the murder of Uriah, and the confrontation with the Prophet Nathan. Heavy and formative stuff.
weekTheme: Boys cover, Men confess God will show us mercy How a young man learns to deal with his sin is one of the most important lessons in all of life. Some men hide their sin in pride, thinking they can get away with things and never get caught. Some men hide their sin because they are embarrassed and ashamed, worried about what others will think of them. Some men cover their sin by trying to be good and make up for it by hard work, hoping enough good works can get rid of the bad. None of these ways are helpful or healthy. This week David is going to do the unthinkable. After all the favor, mercy, and kindness God has shown him, he is going to blow it in a major way. He is going to have sex with someone else’s wife, murder her husband, have a child out of wedlock, and try and cover it all up. As a king with almost unlimited power, he thinks he is going to get away with it. But the same God that saw him as a shepherd, saw him in his sin. God is going to confront David through the Prophet Nathan and teach him about repentance, consequences, and restoration. It’s super challenging to be a young guy growing up in the world today. Most people don’t care what God thinks, encourage you to do what you want and not what is right, and tell you to hide things from your parents. But this will only end in disaster. However, there is good news: David also teaches us how to repent, how to rely on God’s mercy, and how to move past our mistakes. You are going to need to learn this more than you can know. How you learn to deal with your sin will determine your destiny. There is lots to learn here so let’s jump in.

days.title: A Slippery Slope
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 11:1-3 In the spring of the year, when kings normally go out to war, David sent Joab and the Israelite army to fight the Ammonites. They destroyed the Ammonite army and laid siege to the city of Rabbah. However, David stayed behind in Jerusalem. Late one afternoon, after his midday rest, David got out of bed and was walking on the roof of the palace. As he looked out over the city, he noticed a woman of unusual beauty taking a bath. He sent someone to find out who she was, and he was told, “She is Bathsheba, the daughter of Eliam and the wife of Uriah the Hittite.”
days.reflection: No man ever really plans to destroy his life. He doesn’t just wake up one day and think, “Today would be a great day to betray my marriage, disappoint my friends, lose my job, and fail in public.” Yet so many men seem to make sinful choices that do just that. Politicians do, pastors do, teachers do, celebrities do, athletes do. Why does this seem to keep happening to men? The answer? Giving in to temptation. The older you get, the more you are going to realize the importance of understanding the reality, dynamics, and dangers of temptation. In today’s reading, you learn a lot about how temptation worked in the life of David. There are 3 things to note about how this snuck up on him. The wrong time. In the spring of the year, when kings normally go out to war… In that part of the world, travel and fighting were difficult in the winter. There would be weather issues, terrain issues, and travel issues that made it hard to fight. But when the spring came, it was time to get busy. The weather cleared out and the men headed out. David didn’t engage in his regular rhythm. This lack of timing set him up for failure. The wrong place. Late one afternoon, after his midday rest, David got out of bed and was walking on the roof of the palace. David should have been on the battlefield with his men, not in the palace on his own. He should never have even seen Bathsheba because he wasn’t meant to be there in the first place. So much temptation happens when we go to places we know we shouldn’t be. The wrong response. As he looked out over the city, he noticed a woman of unusual beauty taking a bath. He sent someone to find out who she was… Being tempted isn’t a sin. Giving in to temptation is a sin. David should have looked away and gone back inside. But he had the wrong response. Instead of pushing back, he pursued. He went to find out more details about this beautiful woman, and lust got into his heart. Wrong time, wrong place, wrong response. Watch out for this deadly combination in your life. You may think that what happened to David would never happen to you. You aren’t a king with a palace after all. But these dynamics are universal. You may be on a computer at a friend’s house with no parents around, and boom, the same temptation. You may be on a school or sports trip and boom, the same temptation. Knowing how temptation works is half the battle. Be careful; don’t mess around with sin. Learn to fight back hard against temptation. Greater and godlier men than you have fallen into sin. Wake up, watch up, and learn from the failures of others.
questions: What are 2 or 3 of the biggest temptations guys your age face? Where do you see them giving in?
What is the biggest temptation you have faced recently? When did you start to feel tempted? Have you given in?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share about the biggest temptations they faced when they were your age. How were they similar and how are they different than the struggles of this generation?

thoughts.verse: And remember, when you are being tempted, do not say, “God is tempting me.” God is never tempted to do wrong, and he never tempts anyone else. Temptation comes from our own desires, which entice us and drag us away. These desires give birth to sinful actions. And when sin is allowed to grow, it gives birth to death.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you strength to resist any temptation you are facing right now.

days.title: Abusing Power
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 11:4-5 Then David sent messengers to get her; and when she came to the palace, he slept with her. She had just completed the purification rites after having her menstrual period. Then she returned home. Later, when Bathsheba discovered that she was pregnant, she sent David a message, saying, “I’m pregnant.”
days.reflection: Have you heard of the #metoo movement? It got a lot of attention in the world and was a hashtag that went viral on social media. It highlighted women who had been sexually assaulted and coerced by men. Over the course of time, it came out that women everywhere had been abused by men in power. Instead of using their strength to protect women, they preyed on them. Instead of being trustworthy with power, they coerced women with their privilege. When it comes to power and sex, men do so much damage in the world today. King David was the most powerful man in his world. When he called for Bathsheba, she didn’t really have a choice. How could she say no to a man who had the power of life and death over her? She went to the palace, and when she finds out what the king wants, there is nothing she can do. So she had sex with him. And it gets worse. David knew who Bathsheba was. David knew she was married, but he didn’t care. Author Ralph Wilson notes some even crazier details. “It turns out that Bathsheba is related to three people close to David: ---Her father, Eliam, is one of David's thirty elite warriors (23:35). ---Her grandfather, Ahithophel the Gilonite, is David's chief counselor (16:23; 23:34). ---Her husband, Uriah the Hittite, is also one of David's mighty men, one of the renowned group known as The Thirty (23:39).” David is taking advantage of the wife, daughter, and granddaughter of some of his closest friends. That’s how much sex and lust can distort a man’s mind. And then it goes from bad to worse. King David has gotten her pregnant. In his passion and lust, he doesn’t take into account the long-term consequences of his actions. He was thinking of sex, not fatherhood; pleasure, not a national scandal. What is David going to do? Will he repent before God and ask for forgiveness? Will he bring Eliam, Ahithophel, and Uriah in and beg for forgiveness? No. He will abuse his power even further. As you grow up and slowly accumulate power, you want to become a man that God can trust. Your power may not be a whole kingdom, but it can be getting your license and driving on your own, staying with friends without parents around, your first job, and money - all of these things increase your ability to impress and influence others. David lost sight of who God was and what God has given him. It went to his head and he thought that the rules didn’t apply to him. This same thing can happen to you if you are not careful. That’s why 1 Corinthians 10:12-13 warns us: If you think you are standing strong, be careful not to fall. The temptations in your life are no different from what others experience… Power is a gift. Use it wisely or it could destroy you.
questions: Do you think guys your age oppress girls your age in any way? If so, how?
If you could talk with King David, what do you think he would say to you and why?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time he used power wisely and used power badly. What were the consequences of both?

thoughts.verse: Don’t be misled—you cannot mock the justice of God. You will always harvest what you plant. Those who live only to satisfy their own sinful nature will harvest decay and death from that sinful nature. But those who live to please the Spirit will harvest everlasting life from the Spirit. Galatians 6:7-8 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to make you a man he can trust. Ask him to remind you about what you are learning here in these sections of the Primal Path in the days to come.

days.title: The Coverup
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 11:6-27 Then David sent word to Joab: “Send me Uriah the Hittite.” So Joab sent him to David. When Uriah arrived, David asked him how Joab and the army were getting along and how the war was progressing. Then he told Uriah, “Go on home and relax.” David even sent a gift to Uriah after he had left the palace. But Uriah didn’t go home. He slept that night at the palace entrance with the king’s palace guard. When David heard that Uriah had not gone home, he summoned him and asked, “What’s the matter? Why didn’t you go home last night after being away for so long?” Uriah replied, “The Ark and the armies of Israel and Judah are living in tents, and Joab and my master’s men are camping in the open fields. How could I go home to wine and dine and sleep with my wife? I swear that I would never do such a thing.” “Well, stay here today,” David told him, “and tomorrow you may return to the army.” So Uriah stayed in Jerusalem that day and the next. Then David invited him to dinner and got him drunk. But even then he couldn’t get Uriah to go home to his wife. Again he slept at the palace entrance with the king’s palace guard. David Arranges for Uriah’s Death So the next morning David wrote a letter to Joab and gave it to Uriah to deliver. The letter instructed Joab, “Station Uriah on the front lines where the battle is fiercest. Then pull back so that he will be killed.” So Joab assigned Uriah to a spot close to the city wall where he knew the enemy’s strongest men were fighting. And when the enemy soldiers came out of the city to fight, Uriah the Hittite was killed along with several other Israelite soldiers. Then Joab sent a battle report to David. He told his messenger, “Report all the news of the battle to the king. But he might get angry and ask, ‘Why did the troops go so close to the city? Didn’t they know there would be shooting from the walls? Wasn’t Abimelech son of Gideon killed at Thebez by a woman who threw a millstone down on him from the wall? Why would you get so close to the wall?’ Then tell him, ‘Uriah the Hittite was killed, too.’” So the messenger went to Jerusalem and gave a complete report to David. “The enemy came out against us in the open fields,” he said. “And as we chased them back to the city gate, the archers on the wall shot arrows at us. Some of the king’s men were killed, including Uriah the Hittite.” “Well, tell Joab not to be discouraged,” David said. “The sword devours this one today and that one tomorrow! Fight harder next time, and conquer the city!” When Uriah’s wife heard that her husband was dead, she mourned for him. When the period of mourning was over, David sent for her and brought her to the palace, and she became one of his wives. Then she gave birth to a son. But the LORD was displeased with what David had done.
days.reflection: Proverbs 28:13 says this: People who conceal their sins will not prosper, but if they confess and turn from them, they will receive mercy. David is going to learn this the hard way. David has a problem on his hands. A big problem. He has an unplanned pregnancy with someone else’s wife. So he devises a plan to cover up his sin. He brings Bathsheba’s husband home and tries to get him to have sex with his wife so no one knows who the baby’s father is. (They didn’t have DNA tests back then ☺) But Uriah is more noble than David and refuses to do it. So David gets him drunk, and even then he won’t give in. So David decided to kill Uriah by putting him in a death trap during a battle. Uriah is killed and now David’s main problem has been solved. He waits a minute for some mourning for the man he has murdered, then calls for Bathsheba again. When Uriah’s wife heard that her husband was dead, she mourned for him. When the period of mourning was over, David sent for her and brought her to the palace, and she became one of his wives. It looks like everything has gone according to David’s plan. The coverup is complete. He has a beautiful new wife, a baby on the way, and a kingdom to run. But one short sentence is going to bring everything crumbling down. But the LORD was displeased with what David had done. He may have gotten away with things in the eyes of man, but not in the eyes of God. Guys your age often think they can cover up their sin. Whether it's looking at porn, lying to your parents about where you are, trying to cover up bad grades, or going somewhere you aren’t supposed to, there is something in the heart of guys your age that thinks they can get away with things. But the truth almost always comes out in the end. And if it doesn’t, it can start cycles of shame, addiction, and hiding that will go on to haunt you for the years to come. The amazing thing about the Bible is that it is a book that gives men hope when they screw up. The Bible is not some kids’ fairy tale book about happy people living nice little lives. It's filled with stories of men struggling with sex, violence, shame, guilt, rebellion, and failure. But it's also filled with the grace of God that can forgive, cleanse, redeem, and restore. If there is anything you are covering up in your life right now, you may want to confess it and get it out into the light. God will give you more mercy than you can dare to imagine if you don’t try and scheme and cover it up. David got this wrong; you have a chance to get this right. Your call.
questions: What are some of the typical ways guys your age try and cover up their sin? Do you think this works in the long run? Why or why not?
Did you ever try and cover up things you did wrong when you were younger? Would you be willing to share what that was? How do you reflect on that now that you are older?
Ask the person you are doing this study with about a time when they tried to cover up a sin. What was it, and what happened?

thoughts.verse: You have read this verse before, but read through it in light of David's story and your own life. The LORD is watching everywhere, keeping his eye on both the evil and the good. Proverbs 15:3 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to bring anything into the light before God that you may be hiding right now. Ask him for the mercy he promises in his word.

days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 12:1-12 So the LORD sent Nathan the prophet to tell David this story: “There were two men in a certain town. One was rich, and one was poor. The rich man owned a great many sheep and cattle. The poor man owned nothing but one little lamb he had bought. He raised that little lamb, and it grew up with his children. It ate from the man’s own plate and drank from his cup. He cuddled it in his arms like a baby daughter. One day a guest arrived at the home of the rich man. But instead of killing an animal from his own flock or herd, he took the poor man’s lamb and killed it and prepared it for his guest.” David was furious. “As surely as the LORD lives,” he vowed, “any man who would do such a thing deserves to die! He must repay four lambs to the poor man for the one he stole and for having no pity.” Then Nathan said to David, “You are that man! The LORD, the God of Israel, says: I anointed you king of Israel and saved you from the power of Saul. I gave you your master’s house and his wives and the kingdoms of Israel and Judah. And if that had not been enough, I would have given you much, much more. Why, then, have you despised the word of the LORD and done this horrible deed? For you have murdered Uriah the Hittite with the sword of the Ammonites and stolen his wife. From this time on, your family will live by the sword because you have despised me by taking Uriah’s wife to be your own. “This is what the LORD says: Because of what you have done, I will cause your own household to rebel against you. I will give your wives to another man before your very eyes, and he will go to bed with them in public view. You did it secretly, but I will make this happen to you openly in the sight of all Israel.”
days.reflection: At this point, David has settled into his new life. He has a new kid and a new wife, and things seem to be going well. But then Nathan shows up and tells him a story. The thing that is so amazing about David’s response to this story is his blindness to his own sin. Even though he has committed adultery and murder, he can’t see that the story is really about him. Sin has an incredible ability to distort our vision and make us blind. David is angry and enraged, but then Nathan drops the punchline. These are some of the most haunting words in all of scripture. Words you don’t want to hear spoken about you. You are that man! God wants David to know he is not going to get away with this sin. His coverup is a failure and the consequence will be severe. Look at what God says to him. You have gotten arrogant and ungrateful. I anointed you king of Israel and saved you from the power of Saul. I gave you your master’s house and his wives and the kingdoms of Israel and Judah. And if that had not been enough, I would have given you much, much more. Why, then, have you despised the word of the LORD and done this horrible deed? Your violence and murder will haunt you. For you have murdered Uriah the Hittite with the sword of the Ammonites and stolen his wife. From this time on, your family will live by the sword because you have despised me by taking Uriah’s wife to be your own. Your private sin will result in public shame. I will cause your own household to rebel against you. I will give your wives to another man before your very eyes, and he will go to bed with them in public view. You did it secretly, but I will make this happen to you openly in the sight of all Israel.” Dang. This is so heavy. This is so hard. Part of the reason the consequences are so severe is because David was the king. Leaders always carry more responsibility and are more accountable for their behavior. And part of the reason is that he tried to cover up his sin instead of asking for mercy and forgiveness. David’s attempt to control his sin would damage his kids, his wives, his kingdom, and his legacy. All for some sex with a hot girl he saw on a roof some time. NOT WORTH IT. Don’t fall into the illusion that you can control your sin. Don’t believe the lie that you are going to be able to get away with things in the long run. Your sin is stronger than you. It will make you its slave. Your sin will grow and you will have to commit further sins to cover it up. You must surrender your sin to God and ask for his mercy. God longs to be gracious to us. He is slow to anger and full of unfailing love. But if we refuse to turn to him for his heart, we will face the discipline of his hand. You don’t want that. Your sin will not destroy you, but covering up your sin will. Bring it into the light young man. There is mercy and grace, if you will just be honest.
questions: Why do you think it's so easy to spot sin in other people’s lives but not our own? What do you think happens in our hearts to distort our vision and make us miss it in ourselves?
Do you think these consequences were fair for David? Why or why not?
From what you have learned about God so far, how do you think things would have been different if David had confessed his sin and brought it out into the open?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. “And why worry about a speck in your friend’s eye when you have a log in your own? How can you think of saying to your friend, ‘Let me help you get rid of that speck in your eye,’ when you can’t see past the log in your own eye? Hypocrite! First get rid of the log in your own eye; then you will see well enough to deal with the speck in your friend’s eye."
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to remove any blind spots in your life right now. Ask him for the grace to be honest with anything you are struggling with that you may have overlooked.

days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 12:13 Then David confessed to Nathan, “I have sinned against the LORD.” Nathan replied, “Yes, but the LORD has forgiven you, and you won’t die for this sin.
days.reflection: How do you normally respond when you get caught doing something wrong? Do you deny, blame, accuse, or hide? Do you cry, yell, or sulk in silence? Do you cower in fear or puff up with pride? How a man responds when he is confronted with sin reveals a lot about his heart. Can you remember back to when King Saul was confronted by the prophet Samuel? He made excuses, played the victim, and blamed others. He didn’t accept responsibility for his sin, and he got bitter at God. His arrogance and control caused God to take the kingdom from him. But not David. When he was confronted with his sin, he responded immediately with confession and repentance. “I have sinned against the Lord.” There it is. Simple as that. No excuses. No blame shifting. No victimhood. He acknowledges his guilt and throws himself at the mercy of God. Psalm 51 reveals what was happening in his heart. Have mercy on me, O God, because of your unfailing love. Because of your great compassion, blot out the stain of my sins. Wash me clean from my guilt. Purify me from my sin. For I recognize my rebellion; it haunts me day and night. Against you, and you alone, have I sinned; I have done what is evil in your sight. You will be proved right in what you say, and your judgment against me is just. Even though David committed terrible evil, he still had a tender heart toward the Lord. He couldn’t change what he had done, but he could change what he did going forward. David knew that the only hope he had for his life was God's mercy. He could not save himself from his sin, so he humbled himself before God. As you are probably aware by now, you are going to screw up in your life. You are going to make some mistakes as you grow and also choose to sin. You will give into temptation and struggle with shame. But what you do with that sin is the difference between restoration and destruction. You may be tempted to hide. You may try and cover up your sin. You may try and control your sin and think you are big enough to handle this. But this will only cause you heartache and pain. Learn the power of surrender. Learn the power of repentance. Learn the power of confession. There will always be mercy and grace when you confess. It may not take away all the consequences, but it will take away the condemnation. If you learn this now as a young man, it will free you from a secret life of guilt and shame. God is always willing to forgive you for your sin if you ask. Jesus’ whole mission was to take away our guilt, shame, and condemnation so we could walk in confidence and courage before him. Look to Jesus. Learn from him. Surrender to his mercy and you will be amazed at his response. You will hear what the woman in adultery heard. “I do not condemn you, now go and sin no more.” Control or surrender. Restoration or destruction. Be wise and choose the path of life now.
questions: Can you think of 3 examples of Jesus showing mercy to people who were caught in sin in the gospel? What does that teach you about God's heart?
What are 3 steps you can take to make sure you remember what you have learned this week over the long haul?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time that they experienced God’s forgiveness and mercy for their sin.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. If we claim we have no sin, we are only fooling ourselves and not living in the truth. But if we confess our sins to him, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all wickedness. If we claim we have not sinned, we are calling God a liar and showing that his word has no place in our hearts. My dear children, I am writing this to you so that you will not sin. But if anyone does sin, we have an advocate who pleads our case before the Father. He is Jesus Christ, the one who is truly righteous. He himself is the sacrifice that atones for our sins—and not only our sins but the sins of all the world.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his mercy. Thank Him for his grace that covers our sin and makes us new.

extras: WEEK 8 MANSCHOOL FIREPIT AND FRIENDSHIP MAN SCHOOL REMINDER "The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner." - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY: FIREPIT AND FRIENDSHIP Nothing beats the power of men gathered around the fire sharing their hearts. At most youth retreats, they have a moment where people write out their sins and then throw them in the fire. It symbolizes confession, repentance, and forgiveness. This week, you are going to create this moment yourself. Make sure to frame this time well and recap the big idea of the week. Make some space for honest reflection with your son, and have him write out a list of the biggest sins he has committed before God. If he wants to share this, that’s fine, but he may need some space alone. You do the same. You may want to share some personal stories of how God has forgiven you or how you have learned to bring things into the light. Throw them in the fire one by one, and then lead him in a prayer of repentance and reassure him of God's forgiveness and love. Then cook a massive fat steak and make some above-average smores with some weird ingredients. OPTION 2. WATCH THE HEART OF MAN This cinematic retelling of the parable of the prodigal son is interwoven with true testimonials of personal and sexual brokenness. Narrative storytelling and documentary filmmaking combine to reveal God’s compassionate heart. The movie reminds viewers that shame isn’t a barrier to God’s love but rather a bridge to absolute transformation, victory, freedom, and hope. Fellas, this one is potent. https://youtu.be/3qJHd17RQeg
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
